era_context: The Old Kingdom of Egypt is in its final years; the Sixth Dynasty is fragmenting. The Gutian period in Mesopotamia is well-established, with Akkadian political authority completely dissolved. The Indus Valley civilization remains at its peak. In China, the Xia Dynasty (if historical) continues its early development.

events.title: Final years of the Egyptian Sixth Dynasty; political fragmentation accelerates
events.description: The Sixth Dynasty approaches its end, with royal authority severely diminished. Regional governors operate with near-complete autonomy, and evidence of centralized state projects virtually disappears. The transition to the First Intermediate Period is now imminent. Some scholars place the formal end of the Sixth Dynasty around this time, though dating varies by ±10–20 years depending on the chronological system used.
key_figures: Pepi II or his immediate successors (dating and succession uncertain)

sources.name: Turin King List and other Egyptian king lists
Nomarch tomb inscriptions and administrative records

events.certainty_note: The broad trend of Old Kingdom collapse is well-established; precise dating of the Sixth Dynasty's end varies by ±10–20 years depending on the chronological system.
cross_references: Precedes First Intermediate Period c. 2100 BCE

events.title: Gutian period in Mesopotamia; political fragmentation and reduced inter-regional trade
events.description: The Gutian period continues, with Mesopotamian city-states operating independently or in loose coalitions. Evidence of large-scale trade networks and monumental construction is reduced compared to the Akkadian period. The Sumerian King List records Gutian rulers, though contemporary documentation is sparse. This period will end with the rise of Ur-Nammu and the Ur III dynasty around 2112 BCE.
key_figures: Gutian rulers (names preserved in later Sumerian King List, but contemporary documentation is minimal)
events.certainty_note: The Gutian period is attested in later sources; contemporary documentation is sparse, and chronology remains debated.
cross_references: Part of broader Gutian period c. 2154–2112 BCE

events.title: Harappan civilization continues at peak; trade networks remain active
events.description: The Harappan civilization shows no signs of decline during this year. Urban centers remain fully occupied, and evidence of trade with Mesopotamia and the Persian Gulf continues. The civilization's standardized systems and urban planning remain in place. Archaeological evidence suggests stable settlement patterns and continued economic activity.
events.certainty_note: The Harappan civilization's continuity is well-established through archaeology.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Sixth Dynasty's end is contested. Some scholars place it as early as 2180 BCE, while others argue for dates closer to 2150 BCE. The Gutian chronology is similarly uncertain, with alternative chronologies placing the Gutian period at different dates. No contemporary written records from this specific year exist for Egypt or Mesopotamia.
historiographic_note: The historical record for this year is sparse and heavily dependent on later compilations and archaeological inference. Egyptian sources are biased toward elite and royal perspectives. Mesopotamian sources are similarly limited, with the Sumerian King List being the primary source for Gutian chronology, though it is a later compilation with known inaccuracies. The Harappan civilization is known almost entirely through archaeology.

graph_edges.note: Final collapse of Sixth Dynasty led to First Intermediate Period
